coredata.dc:title: Na+-functionalized carbon dots with aggregation-induced and enhanced cyan emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yingfang
Lin, Jie
Wang, Deyin
Wang, Yuhua
coredata.dc:description: A new type of carbon dots that emit blue emission in aqueous state while cyan emission in solid state was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the carbon dots in aqueous state and solid state is 7.6% and 29.2%, respectively. The enhanced and red-shifted emission observed in solid state carbon dots is ascribed to surface state change caused by aggregation. The occurrence of surface state change in solid state carbon dots has been evidenced by concentration dependent steady-state photoluminecence spectra and time-resolved luminescence decay. Surface functionalization by Na+ is beneficial for carbon dots to resist luminescence quenching in solid state. A proof-of-concept study was performed to demonstrate the potential application of the obtained carbon dots as inks for anti-counterfeiting and printing high quality fluorescent images.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Aggregation Induced Emission
Self-quenching resistant

Optical properties of the obtained CDs in aqueous state and solid state
Applications of the obtained CDs as fluorescent ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of the obtained CDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of the starting materials (dithiourea and trisodium citrate) and the obtained CDs; (b) Full scan XPS spectrum of the obtained CDs; (e–f) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s , O 1s , N 1s and S 2p .

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of the obtained CDs in aqueous state (1 mg/ml). The insets are the photos of the aqueous state CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp illumination; (b) Excitation and emission spectra of the obtained CDs in solid state. The insets are the photos of the solid state CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp illumination.

(a) Excitation spectra of the obtained CDs in solid state and aqueous state with different concentrations; (b) Emission spectra of the obtained CDs in solid state and aqueous state with different concentrations.

(a) Decay curves for the solid state CDs detecting the emission at 490 nm and aqueous state CDs with different concentrations detecting the emission at 440 nm (λex = 360 nm). (b) Time dependence and biexponential fits to the luminescence decay of the solid CDs as a function of wavelength (λex = 360 nm).
Emission spectra of obtained solid CDs using different Na sources under 400 nm excitation. Inset photographs are the obtained CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
Illustration of the obtained CDs as inks for (a) anticounterfeiting and (b) printing fluorescent image and text.
Fitting parameters for the decay curves monitoring different emissions from solid CDs (λex = 360 nm).
Carbon dots synthesized by using different starting materials.

row.entry: 1.0 g citric acid + 0.05 g dithiourea + 0.2 g NaOH
1.0 g citric acid + 0.05 g dithiourea + 0.4 g Na2CO3

head.ce:title: Na+-functionalized carbon dots with aggregation-induced and enhanced cyan emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Light-conversion Materials and Technology & Key Laboratory for Special Function Materials and Structural Design of the Ministry of Education, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Light-conversion Materials and Technology & Key Laboratory for Special Function Materials and Structural Design of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Light-conversion Materials and Technology & Key Laboratory for Special Function Materials and Structural Design of the Ministry of Education, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots that show blue emission in aqueous state and enhanced cyan emission in solid state were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, and they potential application as security inks for anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent printing was demonstrated.

A new type of carbon dots that emit blue emission in aqueous state while cyan emission in solid state was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the carbon dots in aqueous state and solid state is 7.6% and 29.2%, respectively. The enhanced and red-shifted emission observed in solid state carbon dots is ascribed to surface state change caused by aggregation. The occurrence of surface state change in solid state carbon dots has been evidenced by concentration dependent steady-state photoluminecence spectra and time-resolved luminescence decay. Surface functionalization by Na+ is beneficial for carbon dots to resist luminescence quenching in solid state. A proof-of-concept study was performed to demonstrate the potential application of the obtained carbon dots as inks for anti-counterfeiting and printing high quality fluorescent images.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as one of the most significant luminescent materials and received increasing attention due to their unique properties, such as low toxicity, low cost, good biocompatibility, excellent luminescence properties, high stability and easy synthesis. Owing to these remarkable features, CDs have been widely studied in diverse applications including bioimaging, photocatalysis, sensors, latent fingerprint detection, light emitting diodes, photovoltaics, etc. [1–8]. However, most CDs only show bright luminescence in aqueous state but weak even no luminescence in solid state, because of the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) like that happened in organic molecular fluorophores [9,10]. The ACQ of solid state CDs has severely hindered their applications in opto-electric device, latent fingerprint detection, luminescent solar concentrator which require solid-state phosphors. Up to now, great efforts have been made to solve the ACQ in CDs [11–18]. A routine strategy to prevent ACQ of solid CDs is to incorporate aqueous CDs in solid matrices, such as in transparent polymer [11–13], starch [14], silica [15], salt [16,17], or montmorillonite [18]. In this way, CDs are kept from direct contact, making their luminescence be retained. However, a common problem for this strategy is that the loading fraction of CDs in solid matrix is usually very low, and further increasing the loading fraction of CDs is always accompanied by significant emission intensity decrease [11,19]. As a consequence, the ACQ problem of CDs hasn't been solved yet, and fundamental solution to ACQ is synthesis of CDs that are able to resist luminescence quenching by themselves. In this work, by taking advantage of aggregation, we have synthesized a self-quenching resistant and bright cyan emissive solid CDs via a simple hydrothermal method. Surface functionalization by sodium is found to be helpful for resisting luminescence quenching. The obtained CDs show blue emission in aqueous state and cyan emission in solid state. Based on this unique property, the obtained CDs were used for advanced anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent printing.
Trisodium citrate (99.9%, Aladdin) and dithiourea (98.0%) (Aladdin) were used as the starting material.

Hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs. In a typical procedure for the synthesis of CDs, 1.0 g trisodium citrate and 0.05 g dithiourea were dissolved in deionized water (14 mL) first, then were sealed into a 20 mL autoclaves and placed into an oven heating at 180 °C for 2 h. After the reaction was over, the resulting aqueous product was filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-filtration membrane. For TEM measurement, the aqueous solution was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 2 days. The solid CDs were obtained by freeze-drying method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained using FEI Tecnai G2 F30 electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a Kratos AXIS Ultra-DLD spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained via the KBr pellet method using a Thermo Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved luminescence decays were taken on an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer, and the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was acquired using an integrating sphere attached to the FLS 980 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of the obtained CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Heteroatom doping can effectively improve and modulate the optical properties of CDs [20,21], and dithiourea contains thiols and amino groups that can be used as heteroatoms sources for “S” and “N'', therefore, dithiourea was used as additive in this work. Fig. 1 a presents the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the obtained CDs. The CDs are uniform and well dispersed with an average diameter of 2–3 nm. HRTEM of the obtained CDs (Fig. 1b) exhibits well-resolved lattice fringes with average interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.25 nm (insets of Fig. 1b), which are close to the (100) and (1120) planes of graphite carbon [22–24], indicating the obtained CDs have graphite-like structures.

The FTIR spectra show broad absorption at 3200–3700 cm−1, confirming the presence of OH group in the obtained CDs (Fig. 2 a). The sharp and weak doublet between 2700 and 3000 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration CH2, indicating that the obtained CDs may contain incompletely carbonized sodium citrate. The strong absorption bands around 1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are assigned to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations carboxylate group (COO−) that inherited from sodium citrate. The sharp peaks at 1300 cm−1 and 1275 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO, and that at 1160 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 are from the stretching vibration of CC. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) of the obtained CDs shows four strong peaks at 284.6, 490.3, 530.9 and 1070.0 eV, attributing to C 1s , N 1s , O 1s and Na KLL , in sequence [24]. The appearance of the Na KLL signal indicates that Na is presented in the final product and may exist in the form of carboxylate according to the FTIR result. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO groups, respectively [24–26]. The deconvolution of O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) resulted in two components centered at 530.9 eV and 532.0 eV, which are assigned CO and CO [24–26]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be fitted into two peaks at ~399.7 eV and ~401.1 eV, which are attributed to the pyrrolic-like N and graphite N, respectively [24–26].The high-resolution S 2p (Fig. 2f) spectrum shows two peaks at 161.7 eV and 162.8 eV, which are assigned to CS2p3/2 C and CS2p1/2 C, respectively [24–26]. From the analysis results of FTIR and XPS, it can be concluded that nitrogen and sulfur elements are successfully incorporated into the current obtained CDs; the surface of the solid CDs forms a sodium salt structure during the carbonization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the obtained CDs in aqueous state and solid state
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a is the excitation and emission spectra of the obtained CDs in aqueous state (1 mg/ml). The excitation spectrum expends from 230 nm to 420 nm and is composed by a weak band with maximum at 250 nm and a strong band with maximum at 350 nm. The former weak excitation band is related to absorption of the π–π* transition, and the latter strong excitation band is likely to originate from n–π* transitions in molecular fluorophores presented on the CDs [27,28]. Under 350 nm excitation, the aqueous CDs emit blue light with maximum at 440 nm. The emission maximum doesn’t change upon varying the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 360 nm. The excitation-independent PL behavior is considered to be related to the uniform surface state of obtained CDs. Under the optimal excitation at 350 nm, the PLQY of the aqueous CDs is determined to be 7.6%. Unlike many CDs whose emission is greatly quenched in solid state, the obtained solid CDs show bright cyan emission under a 365 nm hand-held UV lamp, and have a yellow body color (see the insets in Fig. 3b), implying that they may have strong absorption of blue light. Fig. 3b is the excitation and emission spectra of the obtained CDs in solid state. Similar to the aqueous CDs, the emission of the solid CDs is independent on the excitation light. The maximum emission of the solid CDs remains at 495 nm when the excitation wavelength is varied from 300 nm to 400 nm, and shifts to longer wavelength with further increasing the excitation wavelength. However, compared to the aqueous state CDs, the solid state CDs possess a much broader excitation spectrum (ranging from 250 nm to 500 nm and peaking around 420 nm) and longer emission. In addition, the PLQY of the obtain solid CDs under 420 nm excitation is about 29.2%, which is much higher than that of the aqueous CDs. The red-shifted and enhanced cyan emission in solid CDs are ascribed to surface state change due to aggregation. In aggregates, CDs’ surface states are coupled, and shorten the distance between surface groups may induce electron distribution and self-assembly of monomers CDs, resulting in the formation of new molecular fluorophores and new emitting levels. The self-assembly of monomers CDs in solid state may lead to the expansion of π-π conjugated area, narrowing the energy band of CDs, and as a result, the emission from solid CDs red-shifts to lower energy side [29]. Moreover, the observed red-shift and enhanced emission are very similar to the J-aggregate occurred in the fluorene-based chromophores, in which J-aggregates cause both absorption and emission spectra to shift lower energy side and greatly enhance the emission intensity [30].

To give evidence to support the above deduction that surface state has changed in solid CDs, we have measured the steady-state PL spectra and time-resolved luminescence decay of aqueous CDs and compared them with that of the solid CDs. To avoid inner-filter effect at high concentrations (a commonly observed fluorescence spectroscopy phenomenon that refers to an apparent decrease in emission quantum yield and/or distortion of band shape as a result of reabsorption of emitted radiation) [31], during the steady-state PL spectra and time-resolved luminescence decay measurements, the aqueous CDs were added into a 1 mm quartz cuvette, and the reflected light signal was collected for study. Surprisingly, even the concentration of CDs is varied from 1 mg/ml to 350 mg/ml, either the emission or the excitation spectra barely change, suggesting there exists only one specific luminescent center in the aqueous CDs [32]. However, in sharp contrast to the aqueous state CDs, both the emission and excitation spectra of the solid state CDs red-shift obviously to the low energy side (~65 nm for the excitation spectra and ~55 nm for the emission spectra) (Fig. 4 b), implying that the surface state of solid CDs has changed and additional new surface states with lower energy levels have been formed [32].

Fig. 5 a presents the time-resolved luminescence decay curves and fitted results of the aqueous state CDs with different concentrations and solid state CD. These decays were analyzed by detecting blue emission at 440 nm for the aqueous state CDs and cyan emission at 490 nm for the solid state CDs under 360 nm excitation, respectively. For the aqueous CDs with concentration in the range from 0.2 mg/ml to 350 mg/ml, they decay curves show single exponential decay feature, which further confirms that there is only one specific luminescent center in the aqueous CDs; meanwhile, the corresponding lifetime is decreased with from 6.57 ns to 3.16 ns, which is caused by increased energy transfer among CDs upon increasing their concentrations. In contrast to the aqueous state CDs, the solid CDs show non-exponential decay feature with a much longer lifetime of 17.06 ns calculated from biexponential fits, which again proves that the surface states in solid CDs have changed and the resulted fluorophores/energy states are different from that in aqueous CDs. Fig. 5b is the time dependence and biexponential fits to the luminescence decay of the solid CDs as a function of wavelength, and the corresponding fitted results are summarized in Table 1 , from which it can be seen that the lifetime increases appreciable with decreasing emission energy. The emission wavelength dependent lifetime reflects the complexity of the energy level structures presented in the solid CDs and are an indication of broad distribution of excited states in the solid CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the obtained CDs as fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Benefited from the aggregation-induced and enhanced cyan emission features, the obtained CDs are considered to be applicable as security inks in advanced anti-counterfeiting field. To demonstrate such application, a tag of wexberry was printed on a filter paper with a stamp pad using the obtained aqueous CDs as ink and then was dried. As shown in Fig. 7 a, under the illumination of a 365 nm hand-hold UV lamp, the tag emits bright cyan emission in dry state, and it emits blue emission if being wetted with water first. Owing to this feature, the obtained CDs may find application in anticounterfeiting fields for identification the authenticity of tickets and coupons etc. To further demonstrate the application potential of the obtained CDs, the aqueous CDs were put into the vacant cartridge of a commercial inkjet printer (Canon ts3380) and used as inks for fluorescent printing. A graph of our school badge and a short text contains “carbon dots” were printed (Fig. 7b). Visible text and image could be clearly seen under 365 nm UV lamp illumination. The obtained CDs-ink showed excellent adhesion to the paper, and the printed image and text can still be clearly seen after a few weeks storage in indoor environment, which is important for practical applications. The commercial printing papers always contain fluorescent brightener, making the printing papers emit blue light under UV lamp irradiation. In practical application, to avoid the undesired interference with the light from fluorescent brightener, printing papers or tags with no background fluorescence should be used.

In summary, carbon dots that possess blue emission in aqueous state and enhanced cyan emission in solid state have been synthesized. Compared to the aqueous carbon dots, the surface states in solid carbon dots have changed, which have been evidenced by the facts that the emission of solid carbon dots is red-shifted and greatly enhanced. The surface states of solid carbon dots have broad distribution of excited states/energy levels, causing the lifetime of CDs’ cyan emission be varied upon changing the detected wavelength. Plenty of sodium carboxylate functionalized on surface of carbon dots may have effect of restraining π-π stacking interactions, resulting in the obtained carbon dots retain luminescence in solid state. Moreover, the obtained carbon dots have shown great application potential as ink for anti-counterfeit and printing high quality fluorescent images.

Yingfang Tao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Jie Lin: Data curation, Software. Deyin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yuhua Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 51602137, U1905213), Lanzhou Chengguan Science and Technology Bureau (grant no. 2018KJGG0097) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2019-87).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular fluorescence in citric acid based carbon dots
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Schneider, C. J. Reckmeier, Y. Xiong, M. von Seckendorff, A. S. Susha, P. Kasak, A. L. Rogach, Molecular Fluorescence in Citric Acid Based Carbon Dots, J. Phys. Chem. C 121 (2017) 2014-2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ba, L. Zhang, Y. Yin, F. Jiang, P. Jiang, Y. Liu, Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 565 (2020) 77–85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: H- and J-aggregation of fluorene-based chromophores
J. Phys. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Deng, W. Yuan, Z. Jia, G. Liu, H- and J‑Aggregation of Fluorene-Based Chromophores, J. Phys. Chem. B 118 (2014) 14536−14545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect in fluorescence spectroscopy: as a problem and as a solution
J. Photoch. Photobio. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. K. Panigrahi, A. K. Mishra, Inner filter effect in fluorescence spectroscopy: As a problem and as a solution, J. Photoch. Photobio. C 41 (2019) 100318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color emission polymer carbon dots with quench-resistant solid-state fluorescence
Adv. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shao, S. Zhu, H. Liu, Y. Song, S. Tao, B. Yang, Full-Color Emission Polymer Carbon Dots with Quench-Resistant Solid-State Fluorescence, Adv. Sci. (2017) 1700395.
